attributes.stem:
A 73-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a six-hour history of severe generalised abdominal pain associated with bloody diarrhoea. He reports similar episodes of post-prandial pain over the last few months, but never this severe or long-lasting. His medical history includes obesity, ischaemic heart disease, obstructive sleep apnoea, hypertension and dyslipidaemia. His regular medications include aspirin, atorvastatin, ramipril and metoprolol, though he admits poor compliance. He has a 30 pack-year smoking history and recently completed a course of amoxicillin for a chest infection.

On examination, he appears distressed. His pulse rate is 100/min and irregular, blood pressure 140/90 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 37.4°C. There is mild tenderness in the left iliac fossa, but the rest of the abdomen is soft. Rectal examination shows some soft stool and fresh blood.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with severe abdominal pain and bloody diarrhoea in the context of multiple cardiovascular risk factors (obesity, ischaemic heart disease, hypertension, dyslipidaemia, and smoking) and an irregular heart rhythm suggestive of atrial fibrillation. The combination of these features strongly points to mesenteric ischaemia as the most likely diagnosis.

Mesenteric ischaemia classically presents with pain disproportionate to physical examination findings. The patient's history of post-prandial abdominal pain (intestinal angina) suggests chronic mesenteric ischaemia that has now progressed to acute ischaemia. The irregular heart rhythm indicates possible atrial fibrillation, which is a significant risk factor for embolic mesenteric ischaemia. The bloody diarrhoea occurs as the ischaemic bowel mucosa sloughs off.

Clostridioides difficile colitis could be considered given the recent antibiotic use and bloody diarrhoea. However, C. difficile typically causes watery diarrhoea rather than bloody diarrhoea, and the severe generalised abdominal pain would be unusual. The pain in C. difficile colitis is usually less severe and more colicky in nature.

Inflammatory bowel disease can present with bloody diarrhoea and abdominal pain. However, new-onset inflammatory bowel disease at 73 years of age is uncommon. Additionally, the symptoms of inflammatory bowel disease typically develop gradually over weeks to months rather than acutely over hours.

Diverticulitis typically presents with left lower quadrant pain, fever, and altered bowel habits. While this patient does have left iliac fossa tenderness, diverticulitis is not typically associated with significant bloody diarrhoea. The generalised nature of the pain also makes diverticulitis less likely.

Colovesical fistula is an abnormal connection between the colon and bladder, usually resulting from diverticulitis, colorectal cancer, or Crohn's disease. It typically presents with pneumaturia (air in the urine), fecaluria (fecal material in the urine), and recurrent urinary tract infections. Bloody diarrhoea and severe abdominal pain would not be typical presenting features.
learningPoints: Mesenteric ischaemia typically presents with severe abdominal pain disproportionate to physical examination findings in patients with vascular risk factors.
Bloody diarrhoea with severe abdominal pain in a patient with cardiovascular risk factors should raise suspicion for mesenteric ischaemia.
Atrial fibrillation is a significant risk factor for embolic mesenteric ischaemia.
references: FitzGerald JF, Hernandez Iii LO. Ischemic colitis. Clin Colon Rectal Surg. 2015 Jun;28(2):93-8. doi: 10.1055/s-0035-1549099. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4442720/
Trotter JM, Hunt L, Peter MB. Ischaemic colitis. BMJ 2016. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28007701/

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
